---
title: "Should We Use LLMs During Research?"
date: 2026-04-01
---

# Should We Use LLMs During Research?

LLMs are now ubiquitous. Almost everyone uses LLMs everywhere. Even if I try to avoid using LLMs, sometimes I have to use them.
LLMs can give you the answer to almost every question in several seconds. It is like a drug that makes you addicted to the feeling of "knowing everything", but we should know that it
is not us who know everything. So, how about for researches? can LLMs really help to do research?

Maybe I should change the title to "How to Use LLMs During Research?", because for now, it is really hard
to not use LLMs at all. So let us assume that we should use LLMs during research and discuss how to use them.
To do this, we should reach a consensus that LLMs do not output the true answer; they can only output answers
that "look true", so for every answer, we should check it carefully and keep this question in mind:
"Is it a true answer, or does it just look true?"

So, how should we use LLMs during research? I will only focus on the domain I am familiar with, such as applied mathematics and machine learning.
For applied mathematics, if you do theoretical research, e.g., you need to provide rigorous proofs, then it is for sure that you should use LLMs
carefully. You should check every answer, and at least for me, the proof ideas of LLMs, most of the time, are very strange, so I need to spend much more time
checking their answers, and I would rather do it myself. But for some empirical parts, such as experiments on synthetic datasets and on real-world datasets, that is, when we need
to write some code, LLMs can be a useful tool, since sometimes we do not require very "beautiful" code, as long as it is correct and works well.
And for me, it is easier to read LLMs' code than their proofs.

For some code-based research, such as building a complex machine learning model or other systems, we should write code ourselves. Because again, LLMs can only output "answers that look like the truth".
For example, if you tell LLMs to compute a distance, e.g., $\lvert x_i - x_j \rvert^2$, if you do not specify it clearly, LLMs will compute the distance as $\lvert x_i - x_j \rvert$. Maybe you will say you
can fix it by giving a very detailed prompt, but you cannot specify everything, and even if you give a very detailed prompt, LLMs sometimes do not follow the
prompt, so it is essential to check the code very carefully, because, like proofs, one small mistake in code, even if there is no bug, can make the results very different.
So here I'll provide an alternative idea for code-based research: you can code by yourself first, then provide the code to LLMs and let them
check it. What is the difference? Well, language is very ambiguous. Like I said, even if you detail everything, LLMs may misunderstand you. So rather than letting LLMs generate code from language, why not
just let LLMs check the code directly, since code is a clearer prompt than language?

So the conclusion is very clear: for some researchers, such as mathematicians or theoretical physicists, or other researchers who need rigorous proofs in their research,
using LLMs is less efficient. While for other code-based research, LLMs can help to some extent if we give a very detailed and clear prompt, such as by giving the code directly.
But we should remember that the answers of LLMs are not our answers; the knowledge belongs to LLMs, not to us. We should not pretend to know the answer and deceive ourselves,
especially in research, and we should treat each answer from LLMs carefully.
